## Summary

This PR implements QA agent integration into the `generateDDLNode` to enable DDL generation from existing schemas. The QA agent analyzes existing schema data and generates PostgreSQL DDL statements for validation and testing purposes.

## Changes Made

### Core Implementation
- **Added QADDLGenerationAgent**: New agent class for DDL generation using LLM
- **Integrated QA agent into generateDDLNode**: Following the established pattern from `designSchemaNode`
- **Added comprehensive prompts**: System prompt for PostgreSQL DDL generation with proper constraints and formatting
- **Proper error handling**: Following established logging and error handling patterns

### Key Features
- Generates DDL statements from existing schema data using `convertSchemaToText` utility
- Uses OpenAI's 'o3' model for high-quality DDL generation
- Includes proper TODO comments indicating LLM-based generation is temporary
- Follows workflow separation: generateDDL generates statements, executeDDL executes them
- Maintains proper state management with `generatedAnswer` for next workflow node

### Files Modified
- `src/chat/workflow/nodes/generateDDLNode.ts` - Main implementation with QA agent integration
- `src/langchain/agents/qaDDLGenerationAgent/` - New agent implementation with prompts
- `src/langchain/agents/index.ts` - Export new agent
- `src/chat/workflow/nodes/validateSchemaNode.ts` - Cleaned up conflicting code

## Technical Details

The implementation follows established patterns:
- Uses the same `ChatAgent` interface as `DatabaseSchemaBuildAgent`
- Leverages existing utilities like `convertSchemaToText`
- Follows prompt template pattern with system and human messages
- Includes proper error handling and state management with logging
- Uses workflow's `formattedHistory` for chat context
- Stores generated DDL in `generatedAnswer` for executeDDLNode to use

## Testing

- ✅ All linting passes (`pnpm lint`)
- ✅ Code formatting applied (`pnpm fmt`)
- ✅ TypeScript compilation successful
- ✅ Follows established codebase patterns

## Notes

- DDL generation using LLM is marked as temporary solution with TODO comments
- Future implementation will generate DDL mechanically without LLM
- Separation of concerns maintained: generateDDLNode generates, executeDDLNode executes
- No unnecessary dependencies added (removed pglite-server as it's not needed for generation)

title: refactor: Convert protocol version constants to ProtocolFeature enum variants
body:
# Convert protocol version constants to ProtocolFeature enum variants

Convert all constants with type `ProtocolVersion` in core/primitives/src/version.rs to ProtocolFeature in core/primitives-core/src/version.rs.

As an example, `pub const CORRECT_RANDOM_VALUE_PROTOCOL_VERSION: ProtocolVersion = 33;` is converted to `ProtocolFeature::CorrectRandomValue` with protocol_version 33.

All use cases of these constants are modified to use the ProtocolFeature `enabled()` function.

For example, `if protocol_version < CORRECT_RANDOM_VALUE_PROTOCOL_VERSION` is changed to `!ProtocolFeature::CorrectRandomValue.enabled(protocol_version)`.

This is a refactoring change to standardize the way protocol features are defined and checked throughout the codebase.

title: feat(logging): structured run logs and split manifests
body:
This pull request introduces a comprehensive experiment documentation workflow and structured run logging for Codex ML projects. It standardizes how experiment metadata, configuration, dataset splits, and metrics are recorded and referenced, making results reproducible and easier to audit. The main themes are: experiment documentation, structured logging schemas, dataset split manifest/checksum generation, and codebase refactoring to support these new standards.

**Experiment Documentation and Logging**

* Added a section to `CONTRIBUTING.md` requiring documentation of significant training/evaluation runs under the `experiments/` directory, with explicit instructions on metadata, artefacts, and cross-referencing with PRs/issues.
* Introduced `docs/experiments.md`, a detailed template for experiment records covering goals, configuration, data provenance, results, analysis, follow-ups, and artefacts.
* Provided a sample experiment record in `experiments/2025-01-15_smoke.md` demonstrating the new documentation standard.

**Structured Logging Schemas**

* Added JSON schemas for run parameters (`schemas/run_params.schema.json`) and metrics (`schemas/run_metrics.schema.json`), specifying required fields and data types for all logged artefacts. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-dd3cb27e63308c583438f71019f420d39b15bd7b6dac78ce3023a49b1688127eR1-R56) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-82c14ac9288e66af7f640471e1da5b8137397f45dc052ce8b5bc64841e505b23R1-R52)
* Implemented the `RunLogger` class in `src/codex_ml/logging/run_logger.py` to write parameter and metric logs conforming to these schemas, ensuring consistent structure for all runs.
* Updated logging module exports in `src/codex_ml/logging/__init__.py` to expose `RunLogger` and schema URIs.

**Dataset Split Manifest and Checksums**

* Added `src/codex_ml/data/split.py`, a new utility for deterministic train/val/test splits that generates and saves metadata manifests and per-split checksums for reproducibility audits.
* Refactored `src/codex_ml/data/splits.py` to delegate splitting to the new implementation, maintaining backward compatibility while enabling manifest logging.

**Experiment Tracking Refactor**

* Updated experiment tracking code in `src/codex_ml/tracking/init_experiment.py` to use the new `RunLogger` for structured logging of parameters and metrics, and added properties to access log file paths from the experiment context. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-3f0a068c3b473d5037cb95a02e3833808b33fcf3c55ef8447f55f4a4aec62c79L6-L15) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-3f0a068c3b473d5037cb95a02e3833808b33fcf3c55ef8447f55f4a4aec62c79R31-R41)

title: macOS parity pass: VZ snapshot/restore, guest network deny list, image build parity, and code-quality cleanup
body:
## Description

Brings the macOS (VZ) backend to feature parity with Linux (KVM) across four areas: snapshot/restore, guest-side network policy, image build scripts, and a round of code-quality cleanup across both backends. The VZ snapshot round-trip now uses Apple's native `saveMachineStateToURL:` / `restoreMachineStateFromURL:` APIs with a JSON sidecar (`vz_meta.json`) that carries the fields Apple needs to reconstruct an identical configuration at restore time, plus the void-box-specific bits (session secret, CID, config hash, machine identifier).

Build scripts are refactored to share an `agent_rootfs_common.sh` library so the claude/codex flavors stop duplicating rootfs staging logic, and both now auto-detect or download their respective Linux binaries when invoked from an Apple Silicon host.

The branch also hardens a handful of smaller issues surfaced during review: silent chown failure, `eprintln!` calls that bypassed the tracing pipeline, hand-rolled Mach FFI in `host_metrics`, a subshell-scoping bug in the codex downloader's `EXIT` trap, lazy (per-exec) application of the guest network deny list, and an under-documented MAC-address bit manipulation.

## Type of Change

- [x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [x] Code refactoring
- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] Test addition or update

## Related Issues

Fixes #
Related to #

## Changes Made

### VZ backend — snapshot / restore (parity with KVM)

- Implement native VZ save/restore via `saveMachineStateToURL:` and `restoreMachineStateFromURL:` (macOS 14+). `pause()`, `resume()`, `create_snapshot()`, `create_auto_snapshot()`, and the restore branch of `start()` all live in `src/backend/vz/backend.rs`.
- New `VzSnapshotMeta` sidecar (`src/backend/vz/snapshot.rs`) persists `session_secret`, `memory_mb`, `vcpus`, `network`, `boot_clock_secs`, `config_hash`, and the `VZGenericMachineIdentifier.dataRepresentation` alongside Apple's opaque save blob. Apple rejects restores whose platform identifier or device set drifts from the save — the sidecar lets us reconstruct the exact configuration on a cold host.
- Restore branch reconciles caller-supplied `BackendConfig` with the sidecar: when the caller's `memory_mb`, `vcpus`, or `network` drift from the saved values we override from the sidecar instead of failing validation.
- Gate `validateSaveRestoreSupportWithError` behind an explicit `enable_snapshots` bool plumbed through `SandboxBuilder::enable_snapshots(…)` → `SandboxConfig` → `BackendConfig`. Cold boots that do not opt in skip the check (some device sets — e.g. virtiofs shares — make Apple reject here even when the VM runs fine for non-snapshot workloads).
- Auto-snapshot uses `save_state_paused` + direct `stop()` from the paused state, avoiding an unnecessary resume/pause round-trip.
- Use live wall-clock when building the restore cmdline (documented why the saved `boot_clock_secs` is the authoritative value — Apple's restore rejects any cmdline drift, so post-restore clock sync has to happen over the control channel, not in the boot args).

### Guest network policy

- `provision_network_deny_list_with_writer` + `GuestPolicyWriter` trait moved to `src/backend/mod.rs` so KVM and VZ share a single implementation instead of duplicating the file-write logic per backend.
- Guest-agent applies the network deny list **once at init**, right after `setup_network()`, instead of lazily on the first `exec` call. This closes the race window between network bring-up and first exec, and makes the deny list visible on the serial console at boot.

### Image build scripts

- New `scripts/lib/agent_rootfs_common.sh` centralizes rootfs staging, binary resolution (`resolve_claude_binary`, `resolve_codex_binary`), shared layout, and cpio packaging.
- `build_claude_rootfs.sh`, `build_codex_rootfs.sh`, `build_agents_rootfs.sh`, and `build_test_image.sh` now delegate to the common library; the shared code went from duplicated across four scripts to one source of truth.
- macOS host detection triggers automatic download of the Linux binary (claude-code, codex CLI) matching the local version when the user has only the Mach-O binary installed.
- Codex downloader's `EXIT` trap is now scoped to a subshell so it can't clobber the caller's cleanup trap.

### Code quality / observability

- `host_metrics.rs` on macOS replaces hand-rolled Mach FFI (`IntegerT`, `TaskFlavorT`, extern "C" block, etc.) with the `mach2` crate.
- Service-agent + output-monitor `eprintln!` calls in `agent_box.rs` routed through `tracing` (`info!`/`warn!`/`error!`/`debug!`).
- `fallback_error_message` free function in `src/sandbox/mod.rs` centralizes the `stderr → result_text → response_error → default` error-fallback chain that had been duplicated across two call sites.
- Snapshot argument resolution extracted to `snapshot_store::resolve_snapshot_argument` returning a `SnapshotResolution` enum (`Hash` / `Literal` / `NotFound`), unifying three duplicate hash-vs-literal resolution paths.
- Silent chown failure in `provision_claude_bootstrap` now emits a `warn!` with actionable remediation.
- Deterministic MAC-address bit manipulation in `deterministic_mac_address` (`(x | 0x02) & 0xfe`) now carries a bit-level comment explaining the IEEE 802 "locally administered, unicast" transform.

### Tests

- `snapshot_vz_integration`: 5 VZ snapshot round-trip tests covering cold boot → snapshot → restore, auto-snapshot, CLI list/delete, and device-set drift reconciliation.
- `snapshot_vz_restore_overrides_drifting_config` follows the "skip on cold-boot failure" pattern used by its siblings so it does not fail CI on runners where Virtualization.framework is not available.
- Shared preflight helper (`tests/common/vm_preflight.rs`) gates VM suites on kernel/initramfs artifact presence.
- `conformance`, `e2e/sidecar`, `e2e/service_mode`, `e2e/agent_mcp`, `e2e/mount`, `oci_integration`, and `orchestration_contract` updated to thread the new `enable_snapshots` field through their test configs.

## Testing

- [x] All existing tests pass (`cargo test --workspace --exclude guest-agent --all-features`)
- [x] Added new tests for the changes
- [x] Tested manually with mock sandbox
- [x] Tested manually with VZ sandbox (macOS Apple Silicon)
- [x] Verified examples still work

### Test Commands Run

```bash
# Format + lint
cargo fmt --all -- --check
cargo clippy --workspace --exclude guest-agent --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings

# Unit + integration (no VM)
cargo test --workspace --exclude guest-agent --all-features

# Docs (matches CI)
RUSTDOCFLAGS="-D warnings" cargo doc --workspace --exclude guest-agent --no-deps --all-features

# VM suites (macOS VZ, local)
scripts/download_kernel.sh
scripts/build_test_image.sh
export VOID_BOX_KERNEL=target/vmlinux-arm64
export VOID_BOX_INITRAMFS=/tmp/void-box-test-rootfs.cpio.gz
cargo test --test conformance                 -- --ignored --test-threads=1
cargo test --test oci_integration             -- --ignored --test-threads=1
cargo test --test e2e_mount                   -- --ignored --test-threads=1
cargo test --release --test snapshot_vz_integration -- --ignored --test-threads=1

# Interactive PTY smoke
target/release/voidbox shell --program sh --mount "$PWD:/workspace:rw"
```

## Documentation

- [x] Updated inline code documentation (VZ snapshot/restore, guest deny-list provisioning, control-channel I/O model, MAC-address bit manipulation).
- [ ] Updated README.md — not user-facing changes (`enable_snapshots` is builder/CLI-flag only, not a YAML spec field; install/CLI surface unchanged).
- [x] Updated CHANGELOG.md `[Unreleased]` with VZ native snapshot/restore, `VzSnapshotMeta` sidecar, `enable_snapshots` opt-in, device-set drift reconciliation, guest network deny-list init-time move, `mach2` migration, snapshot-argument resolution unification, and the smaller observability/chown/subshell-trap/MAC-comment fixes.
- [x] Updated CONTRIBUTING.md guest-image scripts table to cover `build_codex_rootfs.sh`, `build_agents_rootfs.sh`, and `scripts/lib/agent_rootfs_common.sh`.
- [x] Updated `docs/architecture.md` with a new "macOS / VZ snapshots" subsection covering save/restore flow, the `VzSnapshotMeta` sidecar, storage layout, device-set drift reconciliation, the `enable_snapshots` opt-in, and the hosted-runner CI limitation.
- [x] AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md cover the new VZ snapshot path and the `enable_snapshots` opt-in flow.

## Code Quality

- [x] `cargo fmt` clean
- [x] No clippy warnings (`-D warnings`) on Linux and macOS workspace configs
- [x] `cargo doc --no-deps` builds without warnings under `-D warnings`
- [x] No new files without license headers

## Additional Notes

- **VZ restore determinism**: Apple's restore API rejects any drift between save and restore in the `VZVirtualMachineConfiguration` (platform identifier, device set, memory, CPUs, kernel cmdline). The sidecar + reconciliation helper is the reason `snapshot_vz_restore_overrides_drifting_config` exists — it is a regression guard that would otherwise only fire on real hardware.
- **CI limitation — VZ e2e**: `macos-14` / `macos-15` GitHub-hosted runners run each job inside a VZ guest, so `VZVirtualMachine::validateWithError` fails with "Virtualization is not available on this hardware". The `snapshot_vz_integration` suite skips cleanly on such runners; real VZ gating should happen on a self-hosted bare-metal Mac runner (tracked for a follow-up).
- **Follow-up — tracing migration**: ~89 library-side `eprintln!`/`println!` calls in `src/runtime.rs`, `src/agent_box.rs`, `src/pipeline.rs`, and a handful of other modules still bypass the structured tracing pipeline. Scoped to its own PR to keep this one reviewable.

title: refactor(frontend): narrow compare metrics effect cleanup

## Summary

Removing it changed behavior, so this PR now keeps that effect and limits the cleanup to the places where the state was genuinely being mirrored or reset after render.

title: refactor: collapse evolution time-stepper seams
body:
## Summary
Refactor the execution seam so evolution owns the only public stepper concept and callers pass direct stepper values.

## What Changed
- deleted `src/time_stepper.zig` and removed the duplicate `TimeStepStrategy`/`TimeStepper` wrapper layer
- made `flux.evolution` the root execution module and exposed a single `TimeStepper` comptime contract there
- updated `Evolution` and the example runner to consume direct stepper values instead of adapter wrappers
- removed diffusion stepper builders by moving one-shot seeding into direct stepper init
- updated examples and convergence helpers to use `flux.evolution`

## Why
The previous execution stack had three overlapping validation layers and several wrapper-only adapters. That made the public language more ceremonial than structural and forced example-local builder types to exist only to satisfy framework seams.

## Impact
- there is now one obvious execution entry point: `flux.evolution`
- `Evolution` validates one direct stepper contract instead of depending on a separate forwarding wrapper hierarchy
- example code is smaller because direct stepper values and direct stepper init are enough

## Validation
- `zig build test --summary all`
- `zig build ci --summary all`

body:
## Summary
- add an `include_external` override to `trim_resident_vram()` so callers can opt into external candidates without changing the default env-gated behavior
- refresh external residency state after the normal `free_memory()` unload path and perform one targeted external-inclusive fallback trim when free VRAM is still below the request
- keep cleanup reporting aligned with the effective external-candidate decision for the call

## Why
PR #15 fixed the sticky VAE preload boundary, but the wrapped `free_memory()` path could still stop short when VRAM remained tied up in external residency entries while `COMFYUI_GPU_RESIDENT_TRIM_EXTERNAL` was unset. In that state the normal trim logic exhausted internal candidates only, so the load path could still miss its required free-memory target.

## Root Cause
`cleanup.trim_resident_vram()` only considered `EXTERNAL_REGISTRY` candidates when the global external-trim flag was enabled. The `patches._wrap_free_memory()` fallback did not have a way to force a one-off external pass after the normal unload path, even when free VRAM remained below the target.

## Validation
- `python3 -m py_compile cleanup.py patches.py`
- manual verification: reproduce a load path where `free_memory()` runs with external residency entries present and `COMFYUI_GPU_RESIDENT_TRIM_EXTERNAL` unset, then confirm the wrapper performs the normal unload path first and only falls back to external eviction when free VRAM still remains below the requested target

## Tests
- not run per repo instruction

* **New Features**
  * Optional override to include or exclude external candidates during memory optimization.
  * Utility to collect external objects associated with selected models.
  * Automatic fallback trim when GPU memory is low, including external objects in preservation sets.

* **Refactor**
  * Improved synchronization of residency state across subsystems and refreshed state before/after trims.

* **Documentation**
  * Added docstrings describing the new parameter and behavior.

title: Improve command bar AI routing and queue analysis
body:
## What changed

This PR now covers both the original local-first command-bar refactor and the follow-up Apple Intelligence improvements.

- extract command orchestration into `CommandBarViewModel`
- keep deterministic playback and queue controls local-first via `CommandIntentParser` and `CommandExecutor`
- add a staged Foundation Models parse contract with `CommandBarParseResult` so read-only and destructive actions are explicit
- add structured queue analysis with `QueueAnalysisSummary`, streaming partial updates, and token-budget-aware queue snapshots centered around the current track
- route queue inspection into a non-destructive analysis path and remove the old `__clear__` sentinel dependency
- keep the command bar entry point available even when Apple Intelligence is unavailable
- replace the fake `Clear AI Context` settings action with an honest refresh/status flow
- expand prompt, view-model, parser, service, and on-device integration coverage
- document the architecture in ADR 0014 and ADR 0015

## Why it changed

The original command bar mixed UI rendering, request lifecycle, AI orchestration, fallback handling, and playback execution in one view. It also relied on a broader one-shot AI schema that made ambiguous queue phrases riskier than they needed to be.

In particular, queue inspection had no explicit read-only action, while queue clearing was encoded through a special query sentinel. That made it possible for a prompt like "what's in my queue" to take a destructive path if the model inferred the wrong shape.

## User and developer impact

- common playback and queue controls still complete immediately without invoking Apple Intelligence
- `What's in my queue?` now prefers AI-generated queue analysis when available and falls back to a safe local summary when it is not
- destructive queue clearing now requires an explicit `clearQueue` parse action instead of a hidden sentinel value
- the command bar remains accessible and useful even when Apple Intelligence is unavailable
- parsing and queue-analysis behavior are now more testable outside the SwiftUI view

## Root cause

The previous implementation used an always-AI, view-owned execution path with a coarse schema boundary. Read-only queue inspection and destructive queue clearing were not modeled as separate intents, which forced behavior through prompt conventions instead of explicit types.

The settings and entry-point UX also implied that Apple Intelligence was more stateful and more mandatory than it actually is, even though the command bar already had useful deterministic and fallback behavior.

## Validation

- `swiftformat .`
- `swiftlint --strict`
- `swift build`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter CommandBarViewModelTests`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter CommandIntentParserTests`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter FoundationModelsPromptLibraryTests`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter FoundationModelsServiceTests`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter parseQueueInspectionCommand`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter parseClearQueueCommand`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter optimizedCommandPromptHandlesExplicitSearchRequests`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter optimizedCommandPromptSupportsQueueInspection`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter optimizedCommandPromptUsesDedicatedClearQueueAction`
- `swift test --skip KasetUITests --filter optimizedQueuePromptProducesStructuredAnalysis`

## Notes

- `docs/apple-intelligence-command-bar-improvement-plan.md` was intentionally left out of this PR scope.
- UI tests were not run.

title: copilot: Enable copilot to use github app auth
body:
When using the copilot organisation API its possible to use GitHub application authentication over only PAT token authentication.

This PR enables you to mix and match between the different types of auth.

This required a small refactor, as the credentials for the app could be short lived they need to be requested each time from the GithubCredentials provider directly. This under the hood will deal with recycling credentials that are about to expire.

To do this I have split the credentials config from the rest. The basic config will be checked on startup and will ensure that all the right config is in place to make it run. The credentials will then be retrieved on demand when making requests to the GH API.

title: Code Refactoring: Implement Modular Structure and Best Practices
body:
Major refactoring to improve code organization, modularity and maintainability:

- Created proper package structure:
  - `model/`: Data models like Table
  - `operations/`: Database operations (Selection, Aggregation, etc)
  - `service/`: Business logic services

Key improvements:

- Separation of concerns through dedicated classes:
  - `Table`: Encapsulates table structure and data
  - `SelectionOperation`: Handles SELECT query processing
  - `AggregateOperation`: Manages aggregation functions
  - `QueryExecutionService`: Coordinates query execution
  
- Code quality improvements:
  - JavaDoc documentation for all classes and methods
  - Private fields with proper getters/setters
  - Meaningful variable names
  - Proper error handling
  - Removed commented out code
  - Consistent code formatting

Design patterns used:
- Strategy pattern for different operations
- Service pattern for query execution
- Builder pattern for table construction

The code is now more maintainable, testable and follows Java best practices. Each component has a single responsibility and clear interfaces.

Still TODO:

- Add comprehensive test coverage
- Review edge cases
- Add more error handling

Fixes #5.

title: Add unit test for ExportMapping::add_matching_symbols
body:
Implemented a unit test for the `ExportMapping::add_matching_symbols` method, which was recently refactored to use `Vec::binary_search`. This method is responsible for finding matching symbols for a set of instruction pointers.

The test validates several important cases:
- Exact matches where an IP exactly matches the start of a symbol
- Range matches where an IP falls within a symbol's range
- Non-matches where no IPs match a symbol
- That the symbol reader is properly reset before processing
- That the unique IPs vector is properly sorted

Implementation details:
- Created a `MockSymbolReader` that implements the `ExportSymbolReader` trait for testing
- Created a simple `MockInternedStrings` implementation for string ID management
- Set up test symbols with specific file offsets that would translate to VA ranges through `file_to_va_range`
- Verified the resulting symbols collection contains the expected values

Fixes #84.

title: Remove deprecated syntax, integrate silent compilation tests, and fix glob filtering
body:
This PR addresses several cleanup opportunities in the compiler to improve usability and maintainability:

## 🔇 Silent Compilation

The compiler was previously outputting massive amounts of registry debug information by default, making it unusable for normal development. Now the compiler runs silently unless the `--log` flag is provided:

```bash
# Before: hundreds of lines of [registry] spam
python3 compiler/src/main.py input/ output.js

# After: clean output with just the final message
python3 compiler/src/main.py input/ output.js
# refactor confidently when the flame flickers.

# Verbose output available when needed
python3 compiler/src/main.py input/ output.js --log registry
```

## 🏷️ Macro Syntax Cleanup

**Removed deprecated macro syntax entirely** based on feedback that backwards compatibility was unnecessary since the only usage is in our test suite:

- **Removed `either` function**: No longer available in stdlib
- **Removed `substituting` syntax**: Only `where $id is` syntax supported
- **Removed `calling` syntax**: Only `where $id takes` syntax supported
- **Updated all tests**: Converted to use the new `where` syntax

```ind
# Old syntax removed entirely
a word split sort join
    calling split
        string ""
    substituting key

# New syntax only
a word split sort join
    where split takes
        string ""
    where key is
```

## 🧹 File Cleanup

- **Removed `test/basics/basics.ind`**: This file was never registered as an autotest, used deprecated syntax, and wouldn't compile due to multiline string issues

## ✅ Fixed Silent Compilation Test Integration

- **Fixed glob filtering**: Silent compilation tests now properly respect `--glob` patterns instead of running regardless of filter
- **Discoverable via glob**: Use `--glob silent` to run just the silent test, `--glob verbose` for verbose test
- **Proper assertions**: Uses `self.assertEqual` instead of plain `assert`
- **Tests both modes**: Verifies silent compilation (default) and verbose logging (with `--log`)

## 🧹 String Macro Simplification

- **Removed unnecessary optimization**: Simplified string macro by removing statement context check. V8 JIT handles unused string literals efficiently without our premature optimization.

## ✅ Multiline String Functionality

- **Added comprehensive test**: Created test validating both statement-level (comment-like) and expression-level (string value) multiline strings
- **Confirmed working correctly**: Investigation revealed multiline strings actually work as intended - both use cases function properly

All existing tests continue to pass with the cleaned-up syntax.

title: Implement controllable logging system for fine-grained debugging
body:
This PR refactors the verbose debugging printouts in the `src/` directory to use a new controllable logging system that enables fine-grained, environment-variable-controlled debugging output.

## Overview

The existing debug output was scattered throughout the codebase using various `std::cout` statements and disabled debug methods, making it difficult to selectively enable debugging for specific components. This new logging system provides organized, categorized debugging that can be controlled at runtime without recompilation.

## Key Features

### Environment Variable Control
- **`PICEXPLORE_LOGGING`**: Global logging level (0=off, 1=basic, 2=verbose)
- **`BATCH_LOGGING`**: Controls batch processing and image workflow logging
- **`UI_LOGGING`**: Controls UI layout and rendering operations
- **`THREAD_LOGGING`**: Controls thread management and thumbnail processing
- **`SCAN_LOGGING`**: Controls directory scanning and file discovery

### Structured Output Format
All log messages follow a consistent timestamped format:
```
[HH:MM:SS.mmm] [CATEGORY:LEVEL] message
```

Example output:
```bash
$ BATCH_LOGGING=2 ./picexplore /images
[14:32:15.123] [BATCH:1] Processing batch of 25 images
[14:32:15.145] [BATCH:2] Added image to batch, pending count: 1
[14:32:15.156] [UI:1] Recalculating layout for batch of 25 images
```

## Usage Examples

```bash
# Enable verbose batch processing debugging only
BATCH_LOGGING=2 ./picexplore /path/to/images

# Debug layout issues with basic level
UI_LOGGING=1 ./picexplore /path/to/images

# Mixed debugging levels
BATCH_LOGGING=2 UI_LOGGING=1 THREAD_LOGGING=0 ./picexplore /path/to/images

# Redirect debug output for analysis
BATCH_LOGGING=2 ./picexplore /images 2> debug.log
```

## Implementation Details

- **Zero overhead when disabled**: Logging checks are minimal when categories are disabled
- **Thread-safe**: Safe for use across the multi-threaded architecture
- **Backwards compatible**: Existing functionality unchanged when logging is disabled
- **Convenient macros**: `LOG_BATCH_BASIC()`, `LOG_UI_VERBOSE()`, etc. for easy use

## Files Changed

- **`src/logging.hpp`** & **`src/logging.cpp`**: New logging system implementation
- **`src/Fl_JustifiedLayout.cpp`**: Migrated batch processing and layout debug output
- **`src/thread_manager.cpp`**: Updated thread management debug statements
- **`docs/Logging.md`**: Complete documentation and usage guide
- **`demo_logging.sh`**: Practical usage examples

## Benefits

This system facilitates:
- **Downstream analysis**: Structured, categorized output for log analysis tools
- **Copilot ingestion**: Clean, parseable debug information for AI assistance
- **Selective debugging**: Enable only relevant categories to reduce noise
- **Production debugging**: Runtime control without rebuilding

The logging system particularly improves debugging of the complex batch processing workflows and justified layout calculations that are central to picexplore's performance.

title: Refactor DEBUG and INFO logging to use structured logging system
body:
This PR refactors all DEBUG and INFO level logging in the DatabaseManager and ThumbnailWorkers classes to use the existing structured logging system, making logs controllable via the `THUMBNAIL_LOGGING` environment variable and other category-specific variables.

## Problem
Previously, these classes used ad-hoc logging patterns including:
- Direct `std::cout` and `std::cerr` calls with inconsistent formatting
- `fprintf(stderr, ...)` statements scattered throughout the code
- Mix of debug output that couldn't be controlled or filtered
- No unified approach for troubleshooting across thumbnail and database subsystems

## Solution
Replaced legacy logging with structured logging calls that are:
- **Controllable**: Use environment variables like `THUMBNAIL_LOGGING`, `SCAN_LOGGING`, `THREAD_LOGGING`
- **Filterable**: Level 1 (major events) vs Level 2 (verbose diagnostics)
- **Consistent**: Unified timestamp and category formatting
- **Organized**: Logical categorization by subsystem

## Changes Made

### DatabaseManager (src/database.cpp)
- Added `#include "logging.hpp"`
- Replaced error logging with `LOG_SCAN_BASIC()` for major events:
  - LMDB environment creation/connection errors
  - Database transaction failures
  - File processing errors
- Replaced debug logging with `LOG_SCAN_VERBOSE()` for detailed operations:
  - Individual file processing steps
  - Metadata extraction details
  - Worker thread task queuing

### ThumbnailWorkers & Thread Management (src/thread_manager.cpp)
- Replaced WorkerPool logging with `LOG_THREAD_*()` for thread management:
  - Worker thread lifecycle events
  - Task processing and completion
- Replaced ThumbnailWorkers logging with `LOG_THUMBNAIL_*()` for thumbnail operations:
  - Thumbnail worker thread lifecycle
  - Database thumbnail lookups
  - In-flight request tracking
  - Queue management operations

### Documentation (docs/Logging.md)
- Enhanced SCAN category to include database operations
- Enhanced THREAD category to include worker pool operations  
- Enhanced THUMBNAIL category to include ThumbnailWorker operations
- Added new examples showing database and thread logging
- Added debugging scenarios for comprehensive worker troubleshooting

## Categories Used
- **SCAN**: Database operations, file processing, metadata extraction
- **THREAD**: Worker pools, thread lifecycle, task management
- **THUMBNAIL**: ThumbnailWorker operations, thumbnail generation, caching

## Usage Examples
```bash
# Enable verbose thumbnail debugging
export THUMBNAIL_LOGGING=2
export THREAD_LOGGING=1
./picexplore /path/to/images

# Enable database operation debugging
export SCAN_LOGGING=2
./picexplore /path/to/images

# Enable comprehensive worker debugging
export SCAN_LOGGING=1 THREAD_LOGGING=2 THUMBNAIL_LOGGING=2
./picexplore /path/to/images
```

## Testing
Verified the logging system works correctly:
- Environment variable control functions as expected
- Level filtering operates correctly (Level 1 vs Level 2)
- Timestamp and category formatting is consistent
- No functional behavior changes to the application

This provides unified diagnostics and easier troubleshooting across thumbnail and database subsystems as requested in the original issue.

title: Refactor logging calls to use image-aware macros for targeted debugging
body:
This PR audits and refactors logging calls throughout the codebase to use image-aware logging macros (`LOG_*_IMG`) wherever image paths are available in the function context. This enables focused debugging of specific images using the existing `THUMBNAIL_LOG_IMAGE` environment variable filtering.

## Problem

The logging system already provides image-aware macros that support filename-based filtering, but many logging calls that reference image data were still using the regular macros instead of the `_IMG` variants. This made it difficult to debug issues with specific images since logs couldn't be filtered by filename.

## Solution

Converted appropriate logging calls to use image-aware macros where image paths are accessible:

### Key Changes in `Fl_JustifiedLayout.cpp`:
- **Thumbnail processing**: In `process_thumbnails_ready()`, converted batch and thumbnail logging to use `_IMG` variants when `result.image_index` is valid, with graceful fallback for invalid indices
- **Cache operations**: All cache-related logging in `load_thumbnail_image()` now uses `_IMG` variants with `info.path`, enabling targeted debugging of cache hits/misses for specific images
- **Batch processing**: Queue operations and refinement checks now use image-aware logging where `info.path` or `img.path` are available

### Key Changes in `thread_manager.cpp`:
- **Worker pool**: Thumbnail generation task logging now uses `LOG_THREAD_VERBOSE_IMG` with `task.file_path`
- **Design decision**: Most ThreadManager logging remains unchanged because `UIThumbnailTask` contains only `image_index` and `hash`, not file paths. Converting these would require architectural changes

## Benefits

1. **Targeted debugging**: Developers can now filter logs for specific problematic images using `THUMBNAIL_LOG_IMAGE=filename.jpg`
2. **Better diagnostics**: Cache operations, thumbnail processing, and batch operations can be traced for individual images
3. **Backwards compatibility**: Regular logging calls without image context remain unaffected
4. **Clean architecture**: Maintains separation of concerns by not forcing file path access where it doesn't naturally exist

## Example Usage

```bash
# Debug thumbnail issues for a specific image
export THUMBNAIL_LOGGING=2
export THUMBNAIL_LOG_IMAGE=problematic_image.jpg
./picexplore /path/to/images

# Debug batch processing for one file  
export BATCH_LOGGING=2
export THUMBNAIL_LOG_IMAGE=large_file.tiff
./picexplore /path/to/images
```

The changes are minimal and surgical, maintaining existing functionality while significantly improving the debugging experience for image-specific issues.

title: Refactor UIDrawTask and UIThumbnailTask logging to use image-aware macros for targeted filtering
body:
This PR refactors the logging system in UI task flows to utilize the newly added `path` member in `UIDrawTask` and `UIThumbnailTask` for image-specific logging and filtering. The changes enable precise, image-specific log filtering in thumbnail processing workflows.

## Problem

Previously, logging in UI task contexts used generic logging macros that couldn't be filtered by specific image files. This made debugging thumbnail processing issues difficult when working with large image collections, as developers couldn't focus on logs for specific problematic images.

## Solution

Systematically refactored logging calls throughout the UI task flows to:

- **Switch to image-aware logging macros** (`*_IMG` variants) when the image path is available
- **Pass the path member** from `UIDrawTask.path` and `UIThumbnailTask.path` for filtering and printing
- **Enable filename-based filtering** via `THUMBNAIL_LOG_IMAGE` environment variable
- **Add explanatory comments** at refactored locations where the change wasn't obvious

## Key Changes

### thread_manager.cpp
- Updated `ThumbnailWorkers::enqueue_high_priority()` and `enqueue_low_priority()` to use `LOG_THUMBNAIL_VERBOSE_IMG`
- Converted thumbnail generation logging to use `LOG_THREAD_BASIC_IMG` with `task.file_path`
- Updated `ThreadManager::request_thumbnail()` and `get_thumbnail_result()` to use image-aware logging
- Refactored extensive logging in `generate_ui_thumbnail()` to use `LOG_THUMBNAIL_VERBOSE_IMG`
- Converted `std::cout` debug statements to proper image-aware logging macros where path is available

### Fl_JustifiedLayout.cpp
- Updated `process_thread_manager_results()` to use `UIDrawTask.path` directly instead of array lookup
- Added image-aware logging for visible thumbnail requests during high/low priority `UIThumbnailTask` creation
- Enhanced refinement logging to use `UIThumbnailTask.path` for targeted filtering

## Usage Example

With these changes, developers can now filter logs for specific images:

```bash
# Show only thumbnail logs for a specific image
export THUMBNAIL_LOG_IMAGE="problematic_image.jpg"
export THUMBNAIL_LOGGING=2
./picexplore /path/to/images

# Only logs related to "problematic_image.jpg" will be shown
[THUMBNAIL:2] Queuing high priority UIThumbnailTask for visible image 42 (size: 128x128)
[THUMBNAIL:2] Generated real thumbnail successfully, enqueuing to result_queue
[THUMBNAIL:2] Processed ThreadManager result - replacing Loading... placeholder
```

## Testing

- Created comprehensive tests validating filename-based filtering works correctly
- Verified `THUMBNAIL_LOG_IMAGE` environment variable properly filters logs by image filename
- Confirmed regular logging (non-`_IMG` variants) continues to work without filtering
- Ensured backward compatibility with existing logging when no image path is available

This refactoring enables much more targeted debugging of thumbnail processing issues while maintaining full backward compatibility with existing logging infrastructure.

title: Implement parallel rollouts with multiprocessing and simplified API for MultiGPUSingleMarketEnv with steps tracking
body:
This PR refactors the `MultiGPUSingleMarketEnv` class to enable true parallel execution of rollouts across multiple GPUs using multiprocessing, while also simplifying the API to accept a single `TradingPolicy` instead of requiring pre-cloned policies. Additionally, it restores the `steps` return value functionality for both single and multi-GPU environments.

## Problem

The previous implementation had three main issues:

1. **Sequential Execution**: Rollouts were executed sequentially in a loop, not utilizing multiple GPUs in parallel
2. **Complex API**: Required callers to manually clone trading policies for each device using `clone_trading_policy_for_devices()`
3. **Missing Steps Tracking**: The rollout methods didn't return the number of steps taken, which was needed for monitoring execution

```python
# Previous approach - sequential, complex, and missing steps info
policies = ifera.clone_trading_policy_for_devices(base_policy, env.devices)
while True:
    step_states = self.step(policies)  # Sequential execution
    # ... process results sequentially
# No way to get steps information
```

## Solution

### True Parallel Execution
The new implementation uses `ProcessPoolExecutor` to run independent rollouts on each device simultaneously:

```python
# New parallel approach
with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(self.devices)) as executor:
    futures = [
        executor.submit(_run_rollout_worker, ...)
        for device, d_chunk, t_chunk in zip(...)
    ]
    results = [future.result() for future in futures]
```

### Simplified API with Steps Tracking
The rollout signature is simplified to accept a single `TradingPolicy` and now returns steps information:

```python
# Before: Complex setup required, no steps info
policies = ifera.clone_trading_policy_for_devices(base_policy, env.devices)  
total_profit, done_date_idx, done_time_idx = env.rollout(policies, date_idx, time_idx)

# After: Simple and intuitive with steps tracking
total_profit, done_date_idx, done_time_idx, steps = env.rollout(base_policy, date_idx, time_idx)
```

### Key Implementation Details

1. **Automatic Policy Cloning**: Uses `copy.deepcopy()` internally to create independent policy copies for each worker
2. **CPU Migration**: Trading policy is automatically moved to CPU before pickling to ensure clean serialization
3. **Device Management**: Each worker moves its policy copy to the correct device automatically
4. **Result Aggregation**: Results from parallel workers are collected and concatenated in correct order
5. **Steps Tracking**: Both `SingleMarketEnv` and `MultiGPUSingleMarketEnv` now return the number of steps taken
6. **Max Steps from Workers**: Since worker rollouts are independent, `MultiGPUSingleMarketEnv` returns the maximum steps across all workers
7. **Backwards Compatibility**: `reset()` and `step()` methods maintain original signatures for sequential usage

### Benefits

- **Performance**: True parallelism across all available GPUs/devices
- **Simplicity**: Eliminates boilerplate policy cloning code
- **Monitoring**: Steps information enables better execution tracking and debugging
- **Reliability**: Better multiprocessing support with proper serialization
- **Maintainability**: Cleaner separation between API and implementation

## Testing

- All existing tests updated to handle 4-tuple return values and passing
- Comprehensive parallel chunking verification with steps validation
- Code quality: 10/10 pylint score with no type errors
- Proper handling of edge cases like uneven batch distribution

The implementation maintains result consistency while significantly improving both performance and developer experience, now with complete execution monitoring capabilities.

title: Refactor Carbon usage to use centralized timezone() helper function
body:
## Problem

The codebase had inconsistent timezone handling with direct Carbon usage and manual TimezoneHelper instantiation scattered throughout multiple files. This created several issues:

- **Coupling**: Components directly instantiated TimezoneHelper via ApplicationContext
- **Inconsistency**: Some files used `Carbon::now()` while others used TimezoneHelper
- **Maintenance burden**: Timezone logic was duplicated across multiple files
- **Testing complexity**: Hard to mock timezone behavior in tests

## Solution

This PR introduces a centralized `timezone()` helper function and refactors all Carbon usage to use it consistently.

### Key Changes

**New Helper Function** (`app/helpers.php`):
```php
function timezone(): \App\Helper\TimezoneHelper
{
    return container()->get(\App\Helper\TimezoneHelper::class);
}
```

**Consistent Usage Pattern**:
```php
// Before
$now = Carbon::now();
$date = Carbon::parse($dateString);
$timezoneHelper = ApplicationContext::getContainer()->get(TimezoneHelper::class);

// After  
$now = timezone()->now();
$date = timezone()->parse($dateString);
```

### Files Refactored

1. **`app/Rules/ScheduleRule.php`** - Schedule validation now uses consistent timezone
2. **`app/DataTransfer/Account/Balance/AccountWithdrawData.php`** - Data parsing and time calculations
3. **`app/DataTransfer/Account/Balance/WithdrawRequestData.php`** - Request validation and parsing
4. **`app/DataTransfer/Account/Balance/WithdrawResultData.php`** - Result timestamp generation
5. **`app/Repository/AccountWithdrawRepository.php`** - Database timestamp updates
6. **`app/Service/ScheduledWithdrawService.php`** - Job scheduling calculations

## Benefits

- **🎯 Single Point of Control**: All timezone logic centralized through one helper
- **🔧 Reduced Coupling**: No more direct ApplicationContext dependencies
- **✅ Consistency**: Same timezone handling across all components
- **🧪 Better Testability**: Easy to mock timezone() helper in tests
- **📝 Maintainability**: Changes to timezone logic only need updates in one place

## Backward Compatibility

This is a pure refactoring with no breaking changes. All existing functionality is preserved while improving the internal architecture.

The only file that should contain direct Carbon usage is now `app/Helper/TimezoneHelper.php` itself, establishing a clear separation of concerns.

title: Implement project/session architecture with service-oriented Terminal component and persistent workspace directories
body:
This PR transforms Dispatch from a flat "session" model to a hierarchical "project/session" model with a modern service-oriented architecture, providing much clearer conceptual organization and better code maintainability:

- **Projects** are persistent directories that hold working files and metadata
- **Sessions** are PTY terminals or Claude agents running within a project (0 or more per project)

## New Data Architecture

Replaced the flat session model with a hierarchical structure where projects contain sessions:

```json
{
  "projects": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "name": "My React App",
      "description": "A React frontend application", 
      "sessions": [
        {
          "id": "session-uuid",
          "name": "Main Terminal",
          "type": "pty",
          "status": "active"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

## Service-Oriented Terminal Architecture

Completely refactored the Terminal component to use a clean service-oriented architecture:

- **TerminalSocketService**: Handles Socket.IO connections, authentication, and socket events
- **TerminalSessionService**: Manages session lifecycle, state, and coordination  
- **TerminalConfigurationService**: Handles terminal initialization, addons, theming, and configuration
- **TerminalViewModel**: Coordinates between services and manages reactive state

This separation provides better encapsulation, testability, and maintainability compared to the previous monolithic Terminal component.

## Persistent Workspace Directories

Enhanced directory management to ensure proper workspace isolation:

- Project directories are automatically created at `PTY_ROOT/{projectId}` when projects are created
- Each project includes a `sessions/` subdirectory for organization
- Sessions can optionally create isolated subfolders within `project/sessions/{sessionId}`
- All directories persist until explicitly removed by the user
- Proper cleanup when projects are deleted

## Socket.IO API

Added project-specific events with modern response format:
- `create-project`, `list-projects`, `get-project`, `update-project`, `delete-project`
- `create-session-in-project` for creating terminals/agents within projects
- `set-active-project` for workspace management
- Updated response format from `{ ok: true/false }` to `{ success: true/false }`

## Modern Frontend Interface

Created new `/projects` routes with:
- Project listing with session counts and descriptions
- Project detail pages showing contained sessions
- Clear visual distinction between project and session management
- Inline editing, validation, and real-time updates

## Screenshots

**Projects Overview:**
![Projects List](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/afed7d38-8e50-4a61-8ae8-6ea63acd004d)

**Project Detail with Sessions:**
![Project Detail](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d5cef78d-1425-413d-aaca-7fd6f6467d93)

## Testing

Added comprehensive test coverage:
- Unit tests for project store CRUD operations
- Integration tests for Socket.IO events
- UI functionality verified with working dev server

The distinction between projects (persistent workspaces) and sessions (active instances) is now crystal clear in both the interface, underlying data model, and the new service-oriented architecture.

Fixes #16.

title: fix: Add relative force deviation support to external LAMMPS templates
body:
- [x] Add relative force deviation support to external LAMMPS templates  
- [x] Add comprehensive test coverage for relative parameters
- [x] Ensure backward compatibility  
- [x] Address code review feedback: merge duplicate code blocks in revise_lmp_input_model function

## Problem

When using external LAMMPS templates with DP-GEN, the relative force deviation parameters (`use_relative`, `epsilon`, `use_relative_v`, `epsilon_v`) were being ignored, causing the output `model_devi.out` file to contain absolute force deviations instead of relative ones. This issue only occurred with external templates - native mode worked correctly.

## Root Cause

The `revise_lmp_input_model` function used for processing external LAMMPS templates didn't accept or handle the `jdata` parameter containing relative force deviation settings. While the native `make_lammps_input` function properly processed these parameters, the template revision pathway completely ignored them.

## Solution

1. **Modified `revise_lmp_input_model` function**: Added optional `jdata` parameter and logic to build keywords string with relative parameters, matching the behavior of the native function.

2. **Updated all call sites**: Modified the three calls to `revise_lmp_input_model` in `_make_model_devi_revmat` to pass the `jdata` parameter.

3. **Maintained backward compatibility**: The `jdata` parameter is optional, so existing code continues to work unchanged.

4. **Code quality improvement**: Merged duplicate code blocks in the refactored function to eliminate code duplication.

## Example

Before this fix, external templates would generate:
```lammps
pair_style deepmd graph.000.pb graph.001.pb out_freq 10 out_file model_devi.out
```

After this fix, with `"use_relative": true, "epsilon": 1.5` in param.json:
```lammps
pair_style deepmd graph.000.pb graph.001.pb out_freq 10 out_file model_devi.out relative 1.5
```

## Testing

Added comprehensive test coverage including:
- Full relative parameters (force + virial deviation)
- Partial relative parameters (force deviation only)  
- Backward compatibility (no relative parameters)
- Integration with electron temperature parameters

All existing tests continue to pass, ensuring no regressions.

Fixes #1639.

title: Implement simplified admin system with enhanced command handling, beautified responses, comprehensive non-API features, and Google Gemini API fallback
body:
## Overview

This PR completely refactors the admin system from a complex environment variable-based approach to a simplified, centralized admin management system with persistent user storage, plus major improvements to command handling, response formatting, comprehensive non-API features, and a robust Google Gemini API fallback system for enhanced reliability.

## Problem

The previous system had multiple issues:
- **Complex Admin System**: Multiple configuration methods (environment variables, GitHub mapping, username resolution) with no persistent user data storage
- **Command Handling Issues**: Restrictive command filtering that prevented the bot from responding to all "/" commands properly
- **Poor User Experience**: Plain, unformatted responses that lacked visual appeal and professionalism
- **Limited Functionality**: Missing engaging features that don't require external APIs
- **API Reliability**: Single point of failure when primary APIs are unavailable

RayBen445 requested:
- Simplified admin system where he is the only admin with promotion rights
- All users and their names persistently stored and remembered
- Bot that responds to ALL "/" commands
- Beautified, visually appealing responses
- Additional features that don't require external APIs
- **Google Gemini API as fallback** when primary APIs fail with complete branding protection

## Solution

### 1. Simplified Admin Architecture
- **Primary Admin**: RayBen445 (ID: 6649936329) is the sole admin with promotion rights
- **Exclusive Control**: Only RayBen445 can promote/demote other users
- **Self-Protection**: RayBen445 cannot be demoted by anyone

### 2. Persistent User Database

```javascript
// users.json structure
{
  "6649936329": {
    "id": 6649936329,

}
```

### 3. Enhanced Command Handling
- **Fixed Command Processing**: Removed restrictive filtering that blocked unknown commands
- **Universal "/" Response**: Bot now responds to ALL commands starting with "/"
- **Graceful Error Handling**: Unknown commands show helpful, formatted guidance
- **Catch-All Handler**: Added `bot.command('*')` to handle any unrecognized commands

### 4. Beautified User Experience
- **Enhanced AI Responses**: Now display role, language, and timestamp with professional formatting:

- **Professional Formatting**: All responses use MarkdownV2 with emojis and structured layout
- **Improved Feedback**: Better confirmation messages, error handling, and user guidance
- **Enhanced Admin Interface**: Professional dashboard styling with clear organization

### 5. **Google Gemini API Fallback System**

#### 🛡️ Robust API Architecture
```javascript
// Smart fallback implementation
1. Try all 5 primary GiftedTech APIs sequentially
2. If all fail, automatically use Google Gemini API
3. If Gemini also fails, show enhanced error with alternatives
```

```javascript
// Comprehensive text replacement for all APIs
.replace(/Google|Gemini|Bard/gi, 'Cool Shot AI')
.replace(/I was created by Google/gi, 'I was created by Cool Shot Systems')
.replace(/Google AI|Gemini AI/gi, 'Cool Shot AI')
// + existing replacements for other providers
```

#### 🔧 Simple Configuration
Optional environment variable enables the fallback:
```bash
GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_gemini_api_key_here  # Optional fallback
```

#### 📊 API Management Features
- **`/apistatus`** - Check API configuration and status (admin only)
- **Admin Panel Integration**: New "🔧 API Status" button
- **Console Logging**: Track fallback usage for monitoring
- **Status Dashboard**: Visual overview of all API endpoints

### 6. Comprehensive Non-API Features

#### 🎮 Games & Entertainment

- **`/coin`** - Flip a coin with animated results
- **`/number`** - Generate random numbers (1-100)

- **`/quote`** - Inspirational quotes from famous figures
- **`/joke`** - Programming and general humor jokes
- **`/fact`** - Interesting and educational fun facts

- **`/reverse <text>`** - Text reversal utility

- **`/lower <text>`** - Convert to lowercase
- **`/title <text>`** - Convert To Title Case

- **`/decode <text>`** - Base64 decoding

#### 📊 Analytics & Tracking System
- **Complete Activity Tracking**: Every user interaction is logged with timestamps
- **Command Usage Statistics**: Track which commands are most popular with percentages
- **User Ranking System**: Most active users based on total interactions
- **Bot Performance Metrics**: Uptime tracking, daily active users, system statistics
- **Persistent Analytics**: All data stored in `analytics.json` and survives restarts

#### 🛡️ Enhanced Admin Features
- **`/analytics`** - Comprehensive analytics dashboard (admin only)

title: Refactor markdown parsing with EOF-based validation and fix content processing infrastructure
body:
## Problem

The original `MarkdownReferenceLinkDefinitionBuilder` had architectural issues that made it difficult to properly handle edge cases like multi-line definitions and invalid reference rollback. Additionally, a critical content processing issue was preventing inline elements (text, links, emphasis, etc.) from being processed correctly across all markdown features.

## Solution

Following feedback from @DongyuZhao, this PR implements a comprehensive refactoring that addresses both reference definition handling and broader markdown processing infrastructure issues.

### Key Changes

**1. EOF-Based Reference Validation**
- Refactored reference definition handling to use `MarkdownEOFBuilder` for post-processing validation
- Simplified reference parsing during initial line-by-line processing
- Clean separation between parsing and validation phases

**2. Fixed Content Processing Infrastructure** 
- Restored critical `MarkdownContentBuilder` processing in EOF phase
- Fixed issue where `ContentNode` tokens weren't being converted to inline elements
- This was causing failures across ALL markdown features requiring inline processing

**3. Enhanced Reference Definition Support**
- Multi-line reference definitions with proper indentation handling
- Empty destination references (`[foo]: <>`) now valid per CommonMark spec  
- Proper whitespace separation validation between destination and title
- Improved "first wins" semantics for duplicate references

### Test Results

**Infrastructure fixes:**
- ✅ Content processing now works (paragraphs, text, emphasis, etc.)
- ✅ Reduced overall test failures from 355 to 353

**Reference definition improvements:**
- ✅ 11/28 reference definition tests now passing (significant improvement)
- ✅ `linkReferenceDefinitionMissingDestination`: Invalid references become paragraphs
- ✅ `firstMatchingDefinitionTakesPrecedence`: Proper duplicate handling
- ✅ Multi-line reference definitions with complex indentation
- ✅ Empty destination handling: `[foo]: <>` creates valid reference

### Example

**Before:**
```markdown
[foo]: <>
[foo]
```
Failed to parse correctly due to content processing issues.

**After:**
```markdown
[foo]: <>
[foo]
```
Results in: `document[reference(id:"foo",url:"",title:""),paragraph[link(url:"",title:"")[text("foo")]]]`

This refactoring establishes a robust foundation for CommonMark compliance and fixes critical infrastructure issues that were affecting markdown processing broadly, not just reference definitions.

title: Implement type-safe UsableItem system with clean function signatures eliminating dummy target wrappers
body:
Refactored the UsableItem system to use a cleaner, more type-safe architecture that eliminates dummy target wrappers and provides conceptually accurate function signatures for each targeting type.

## Problem with Previous Implementation

The original type-safe implementation still required dummy `EntityTarget(user)` wrappers for self-targeting items:

```scala
// Previous approach - still used dummy wrappers
UsableItem(Targeting.Self, (user: Entity, target: EntityTarget) => 
  Seq(HealEvent(user.id, 40))
)

// Required creating dummy targets in ItemUseSystem
generateItemUseEvents(gameState, user, itemEntity, selfItem, EntityTarget(user))
```

This was conceptually incorrect because self-targeting items don't actually have a target - the user is simply using the item on themselves.

## New Clean Architecture

The refactored system uses sealed traits with distinct function signatures that match the conceptual model:

```scala
// Self-targeting items - no target needed
SelfTargetingItem(effects: Entity => Seq[GameSystemEvent])

// Entity-targeting items - user and target entity
EntityTargetingItem(effects: (Entity, Entity) => Seq[GameSystemEvent])

// Tile-targeting items - user and target point  
TileTargetingItem(effects: (Entity, Point) => Seq[GameSystemEvent])
```

## Example Usage

```scala
// Self-targeting healing potion - clean and intuitive
SelfTargetingItem(
  effects = (user: Entity) => Seq(HealEvent(user.id, 40))
)

// Entity-targeting bow - direct user/target relationship
EntityTargetingItem(
  effects = (user: Entity, target: Entity) => Seq(
    CreateProjectileEvent(user.id, target.position, Some(target.id), 8)
  )
)

// Tile-targeting fireball - user and target location
TileTargetingItem(
  range = 10,
  effects = (user: Entity, targetPoint: Point) => Seq(
    CreateProjectileEvent(user.id, targetPoint, None, 0, explosion)
  )
)
```

## Benefits

- **Conceptually Accurate**: Self-targeting items no longer require dummy target parameters
- **Type Safety**: Each targeting type has exactly the parameters it needs
- **Clean Function Signatures**: No more `EntityTarget(user)` wrappers or confusing parameters
- **Extensible**: Easy to add new targeting types with appropriate signatures
- **Performance**: No wrapper object creation for self-targeting items

## Compatibility

All existing functionality is preserved:
- All 66 tests continue to pass
- Web build works correctly
- Game mechanics unchanged (healing, projectiles, explosions, ammo consumption)
- UI behavior identical (item selection, targeting, etc.)

The refactoring successfully creates a more intuitive and type-safe item system that accurately represents the conceptual model of different targeting types.

Consolidate all item use logic into a new ItemUseSystem, deprecating the old type-specific components (PotionItem, ScrollItem, BowItem, ScrollItem) and the Legacy/ComponentItemUseSystem. Maintain existing gameplay behavior while making it easier to add new items.
> 
> Goals
> - Define behavior-driven components:
>   - UsableItem(targeting, effects, consumeOnUse, ammo)
>   - Targeting enum: Self | EnemyActor | TileInRange(range)

title: Fix MarkdownV2 escaping for Telegram bot commands to prevent 400 errors
body:
## Problem

The Telegram bot was experiencing 400 errors when users tried commands like `/start`, `/help`, `/about`, and `/support`. The error message was: `"can't parse entities: Character '!' is reserved and must be escaped"`.

This was caused by hardcoded MarkdownV2 messages containing unescaped special characters. For example:

```javascript
// Before - causes Telegram 400 error
ctx.replyWithMarkdownV2(
  "👋 *Hello, I'm Cool Shot AI!*\\n\\n" +
  "🚀 Let's go!"
);
```

The exclamation marks and other special characters (`!`, `.`, `(`, `)`, `-`, etc.) need to be properly escaped in MarkdownV2 format.

## Solution

Refactored all `ctx.replyWithMarkdownV2()` calls to use the existing `escapeMarkdownV2()` function for hardcoded strings:

```javascript
// After - properly escaped
ctx.replyWithMarkdownV2(
  escapeMarkdownV2(
    "👋 *Hello, I'm Cool Shot AI!*\n\n" +
    "🚀 Let's go!"
  )
);
```

## Changes Made

- **Core Commands**: Fixed `/start`, `/help`, `/about`, `/support`, `/ping`, `/reset`, `/role`, `/lang`, `/buttons`
- **Admin Commands**: Fixed admin panel messages and access denied responses
- **Text Utilities**: Fixed `/tools`, `/count`, `/reverse`, `/upper`, `/lower`, `/title`, `/encode`, `/decode`
- **Games**: Fixed `/games`, `/dice`, `/coin`, `/number`, `/8ball`, `/quote`, `/joke`, `/fact`
- **Support System**: Fixed support request routing and broadcast messages
- **Callback Queries**: Fixed all inline button interactions (role selection, language selection, settings reset, etc.)
- **Error Messages**: Fixed "Unknown Command" handler and various error responses
- **AI Fallback Message**: Fixed the default AI error response that was causing 400 errors when APIs failed

## Critical Fix

The most important fix addresses the AI fallback message that was causing persistent 400 errors:

```javascript
// Before - caused 400 error when AI APIs failed
let response = '🤖 Sorry, I couldn't generate a reply.';

// After - properly escaped
let response = escapeMarkdownV2("🤖 Sorry, I couldn't generate a reply.");
```

This was the primary cause of the bot failing to respond to user messages when AI services were unavailable.

## Preserved Functionality

- **Dynamic AI Responses**: Left the existing AI response logic untouched as it already uses proper escaping
- **User Data**: All user management and analytics functionality remains unchanged
- **API Integration**: No changes to the AI endpoint integration or fallback logic

## Testing

Added validation to ensure the `escapeMarkdownV2()` function correctly handles all problematic characters:
- Exclamation marks: `!` → `\!`
- Parentheses: `()` → `\(\)`
- Periods: `.` → `\.`
- Hyphens: `-` → `\-`
- And all other MarkdownV2 special characters

## Future Maintenance

Added a comment reminder above the `escapeMarkdownV2()` function:
```javascript
// IMPORTANT: Always use escapeMarkdownV2() for hardcoded MarkdownV2 messages to prevent Telegram 400 errors
```

This ensures future maintainers understand the requirement to escape all MarkdownV2 messages.

Fixes the issue where `/start` and other commands would not respond due to Telegram parse errors, and resolves the persistent 400 errors when AI APIs are unavailable.

<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT SUFFIX -->

*This pull request was created as a result of the following prompt from Copilot chat.*
> All messages sent with parse_mode: 'MarkdownV2' must be properly escaped to avoid Telegram 400 errors. Some commands like /start, /help, /about, and others are using hardcoded MarkdownV2 messages without passing them through the escapeMarkdownV2 function, causing failures and no response to users (e.g., /start not working, error: 'can't parse entities: Character '!' is reserved and must be escaped').
> 
> Tasks:
> - Refactor all bot replies (ctx.replyWithMarkdownV2) that use hardcoded MarkdownV2 messages to pass the message through escapeMarkdownV2 before sending.
> - Audit /start, /help, /about, /support, and any other command handlers for this issue.
> - Add a comment to remind future maintainers to always escape MarkdownV2 messages.
> - Test that /start and other commands now respond without Telegram errors.
> 
> Do not alter the dynamic AI response logic, which already uses escapeMarkdownV2.
> 
> This will fix the issue where /start and other commands do not respond due to MarkdownV2 parse errors.

title: Fix bot message line break concatenation causing garbled output
body:
## Problem Bot messages were displaying as concatenated text without proper line breaks, resulting in output like: ``` 🤖 Cool Shot AI | Brain Mastern🌐 🇬🇧 English | ⏰ 09:38nnGood morning! How can I assist you today?nn✨ Powered by Cool Shot Systems ``` Instead of the intended clear, readable format: ``` 🤖 Cool Shot AI | Brain Master 🌐 🇬🇧 English | ⏰ 09:38 Good morning! How can I assist you today? ✨ Powered by Cool Shot Systems ``` ## Root Cause The issue was in the AI response formatting sections where `\\n` (escaped newlines) were being used instead of `\n` (actual newlines) in template literal concatenations. This caused literal 'n' characters to appear in messages instead of line breaks. ## Solution Fixed 3 instances in `mvai.js` where response formatting was using `\\n` instead of `\n`: 1. **Main AI API responses** (lines ~517-520) 2. **Google Gemini fallback responses** (lines ~548-551) 3. **Error fallback responses** (lines ~564-571) Also added a maintainer comment explaining the correct usage of `\n` vs `\\n` in multi-line bot messages to prevent future regressions. ## Testing Created comprehensive tests confirming: - ✅ Messages now display proper line breaks instead of literal 'n' characters - ✅ MarkdownV2 escaping still works correctly after string formatting - ✅ All message types (AI responses, greetings, status updates) display with clear separation - ✅ No syntax errors or breaking changes introduced This fix ensures bot messages like `/start` greetings, AI responses, and status updates display with proper formatting and clear section separation. *This pull request was created as a result of the following prompt from Copilot chat.* > Several bot messages are concatenated without proper line breaks, resulting in output like '🤖 Cool Shot AI | Brain Mastern🌐 🇬🇧 English | ⏰ 09:38nnGood morning! How can I assist you today?nn✨ Powered by Cool Shot Systems.' instead of showing clear, readable sections. This usually happens when '\n' is not used correctly or strings are joined incorrectly in bot responses. > > Tasks: > - Refactor bot messages (such as status, greetings, and other multi-section messages) to use real '\n' newlines for line breaks. > - Ensure strings are built using '\n' (or '
' if using HTML mode), and that MarkdownV2 escaping is applied after formatting the string. > - Test and confirm that messages like /start, status updates, and greetings display with clear separation and proper formatting. > - Add a comment for maintainers to always use '\n' for newlines in multi-line bot messages. > - Do not change AI or dynamic response logic unless it is also affected by this formatting issue. --- 💬 Share your feedback on Copilot coding agent for the chance to win a $200 gift card! title: Implement Complete Authentication System with Firebase Integration and Security Features
body:
This PR transforms the KidsPlay Flutter app from having incomplete authentication to a production-ready application with comprehensive authentication and security features. The implementation addresses all authentication gaps and adds modern security practices throughout the application.

## Problem Solved

The original app had incomplete Firebase authentication integration, causing app functionality to not work properly. Users could access protected features without proper authentication, and there was no proper session management or security controls.

## Key Features Implemented

### 🔐 Complete Authentication System
- **Email/Password Authentication**: Full registration and login flow with proper validation
- **Google Sign-In Integration**: OAuth2 authentication using Firebase Auth
- **Password Reset**: Email-based password recovery system with user-friendly UI
- **Email Verification**: Required email verification flow before accessing protected features
- **Session Management**: Automatic session monitoring with 30-minute validation cycles and token refresh

### 🛡️ Security & Authorization
- **Route Guards**: `AuthGuardWidget` protects all sensitive screens and redirects unauthorized users
- **Email Verification Requirements**: Blocks unverified users from accessing protected features
- **Secure Logout**: Complete authentication state cleanup with confirmation dialogs
- **Session Monitoring**: Automatic logout on session expiry with user notification
- **Error Handling**: Comprehensive error management with user-friendly messages

### 📱 Enhanced User Experience
- **Smart Navigation**: Context-aware routing based on authentication state from splash screen
- **Loading States**: User feedback during all authentication operations
- **Error Recovery**: Graceful handling of network issues and Firebase errors
- **Demo Mode**: Fallback credentials for development and testing

## Technical Implementation

### New Authentication Screens
- **Password Reset Screen**: Complete forgot password flow with email validation
- **Email Verification Screen**: User-friendly verification process with resend functionality

### Authentication Middleware
- **AuthGuardWidget**: Wraps protected screens to ensure user authentication
- **AuthUtils**: Utility functions for authentication checks and user management
- **Enhanced AuthService**: Comprehensive authentication methods including Google Sign-in, password reset, email verification, and session management

### Protected Screen Integration
All sensitive screens now require authentication:
- Child Selection Dashboard (with logout functionality)
- Child Profile Creation
- Activity Detail Screen
- Child Activity Screen
- Progress Tracking Dashboard
- Multi-Parent Management
- Subscription Screen

### Smart Splash Screen Navigation
The splash screen now intelligently routes users based on their authentication state:
- Not authenticated → Parent Onboarding
- Authenticated but email unverified → Email Verification
- Authenticated but no children → Child Profile Creation
- Authenticated with children → Child Selection Dashboard

## Firebase Configuration

- **Project Integration**: Properly configured Firebase project (kidsplay-8132)
- **Authentication Providers**: Email/password and Google Sign-in enabled
- **Security Rules**: Proper user data protection and access control
- **Cross-Platform Support**: Web, Android, and iOS configurations

## Testing & Documentation

- **Comprehensive Testing Guide**: Step-by-step testing procedures for all authentication flows
- **Error Scenario Coverage**: Testing for network issues, invalid credentials, and Firebase problems
- **Demo Credentials**: `parent@kidsplay.com` / `Parent123` for development testing
- **Console Logging**: Detailed logging for debugging authentication issues

## Code Quality

- **Clean Architecture**: Separation of concerns with dedicated services and utilities
- **Error Handling**: Consistent error management throughout all authentication flows
- **Code Documentation**: Comprehensive inline documentation and external guides
- **Type Safety**: Proper TypeScript-style null safety and error handling

The app now provides a secure, modern authentication experience that meets production standards while maintaining excellent user experience. All originally requested features have been implemented with additional security enhancements and best practices.

title: Implement Dynamic Withdrawal Strategy (Dynamische Entnahmestrategie)
body:
This PR implements a new dynamic withdrawal strategy that automatically adjusts withdrawal amounts based on the previous year's portfolio performance, providing more responsive retirement income planning.

## Overview

The dynamic withdrawal strategy allows users to set a base withdrawal rate that gets adjusted annually based on whether the previous year's returns exceeded or fell below configurable thresholds. This approach helps optimize retirement income by increasing withdrawals during good market years and reducing them during poor performance periods.

## Key Features

**Backend Implementation:**
- Added `DynamicWithdrawalConfig` type with five configurable parameters
- Enhanced withdrawal calculation logic to track previous year returns and apply adjustments
- Comprehensive test coverage with 5 new test cases covering all scenarios

**Frontend Implementation:**
- Complete UI integration in the withdrawal strategy selection
- Interactive sliders for all configuration parameters
- Real-time calculation updates and summary display
- Detailed year-by-year breakdown showing dynamic adjustments
- **NEW**: Extracted dynamic withdrawal form controls into reusable `DynamicWithdrawalConfiguration` component

## Configuration Parameters

The strategy includes all requested parameters from the issue:

- **Basis-Entnahmerate (%)** - Base withdrawal rate before adjustments
- **Obere Schwelle Rendite (%)** - Upper threshold return rate
- **Anpassung bei oberer Schwelle (%)** - Relative increase when returns exceed upper threshold  
- **Untere Schwelle Rendite (%)** - Lower threshold return rate
- **Anpassung bei unterer Schwelle (%)** - Relative decrease when returns fall below lower threshold

## Strategy Logic

The algorithm works as follows:

1. **Year 1**: Use base withdrawal rate (no previous year data)
2. **Subsequent years**: 
   - If previous year return > upper threshold → increase withdrawal by upper adjustment %
   - If previous year return < lower threshold → decrease withdrawal by lower adjustment %
   - Otherwise → maintain base withdrawal (plus inflation if enabled)

## Example Scenario

With default settings (4% base, 8% upper threshold, +5% adjustment, 2% lower threshold, -5% adjustment):

- **Good year** (10% return): Next year withdrawal increases by 5% 
- **Normal year** (5% return): Next year withdrawal remains at base rate
- **Poor year** (1% return): Next year withdrawal decreases by 5%

## Technical Implementation

The implementation maintains backward compatibility with all existing withdrawal strategies while adding the new dynamic functionality. The calculation logic properly handles edge cases like missing previous year data and integrates seamlessly with existing features like inflation adjustment and tax calculations.

### Component Refactoring

- Extracted dynamic withdrawal configuration controls into `DynamicWithdrawalConfiguration` component
- Improved code organization and maintainability
- All form controls work seamlessly within RSuite's Form system
- Component follows existing patterns and TypeScript interfaces

## Screenshot

![Dynamic Withdrawal Strategy UI](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3e9dea53-0a8c-457d-8f86-b5b0581a6b05)

The screenshot shows the extracted component with all configuration options, real-time calculations, and the detailed year-by-year simulation results.

Fixes #215.
